title: User Manual
text: This manual covers installation, the expected data format, and every analysis module in GenoSuite.

title: User Manual
text:
Installation

Windows installer (recommended)

Go to the Download page and download the latest GenoSuite-Setup.exe.
Double-click the installer and follow the prompts.
Launch GenoSuite from the Start menu. It opens in its own application window; all computation runs locally on your machine.

No separate R installation is required — the installer is self-contained.


Running from source
If you have R installed:
install.packages(c("shiny", "bslib", "DT", "ggplot2", "ape", "vegan",
                   "glmnet", "ranger", "xgboost"))
# install GSbench from R-universe
install.packages("GSbench", repos = "https://mqfarooqi1.r-universe.dev")

shiny::runApp("app")

title: User Manual
text:
Data format
GenoSuite reads CSV or Excel (.xlsx/.xls) files in which:

Each row is one individual / accession / OTU.
One column holds a unique ID (e.g. Ind001).
Optionally, one column holds a grouping variable (e.g. Population), used to colour plots and to compute Fst.
Optionally, one numeric phenotype column (e.g. Trait), used for GWAS and genomic prediction.
All remaining numeric columns are treated as markers, coded as allele dosage: 0, 1, or 2 (copies of the reference/alternate allele).




ID
Population
Trait
SNP001
SNP002
…




Ind001
Pop1
12.4
0
2
…


Ind002
Pop1
9.8
1
1
…



On the Data tab, use the selectors to tell GenoSuite which columns are the ID, group, and phenotype. Everything else is used as markers.






Tip



New to the app? Click Load demo SNP dataset on the Data tab. It loads 36 individuals from 3 populations, 60 SNPs, and a heritable trait — enough to try every module immediately.

title: User Manual
text:
Modules

Data
Import a CSV (or load the demo), preview it as an interactive table, and assign the ID, grouping, and phenotype columns. These assignments drive every other module.


Distance
Computes a pairwise distance / dissimilarity matrix among individuals.

Metrics: Euclidean, Manhattan (city-block), 1 − Pearson correlation, Jaccard (presence/absence), Gower.
Standardise markers rescales each marker to mean 0 / variance 1 before computing Euclidean-type distances.
The result is shown as a clustered heatmap and can be exported to CSV.



Clustering
Builds a tree from the distance matrix.

Methods: UPGMA (average linkage), Ward’s method, complete linkage, single linkage, and neighbour-joining (NJ).
Hierarchical methods are drawn as dendrograms; NJ as an unrooted tree with tips coloured by group.
Export the tree in Newick format for use in other phylogenetic software.




Neighbour-joining tree of the demo populations




Ordination
Reduces the data to a few interpretable axes.

PCA is computed directly on the markers.
PCoA (principal coordinates) is computed on the distance matrix.
Choose which axes to plot; points are coloured by group, with a scree plot showing the variance explained by each axis.




PCA of the demo populations




Mantel test
Tests the correlation between two distance matrices (built with two metrics) using permutations, reporting the Mantel statistic r and its significance, with a scatter plot of the paired distances.


Diversity
Summarises genetic diversity from the allele dosages.

Per marker: allele frequency, minor-allele frequency (MAF), expected heterozygosity (He), observed heterozygosity (Ho), and polymorphism information content (PIC).
Between groups: Nei’s Fst (Gst) per marker and overall (requires a grouping column).
Summary value boxes, distribution histograms, a sortable table, and CSV export.




Distributions of MAF, He, and PIC across markers




GWAS
Performs a genome-wide association scan: each marker is regressed on the phenotype.

PC structure correction: include the first k principal components as covariates to control for population structure.
Outputs a Manhattan plot (with a Bonferroni significance line), a QQ plot to check calibration, and a table of the strongest associations.
Requires a phenotype column.




Manhattan plot from the demo trait




Prediction
Cross-validated genomic prediction powered by the GSbench engine.

Models: GBLUP plus machine-learning predictors — elastic net, random forest, gradient boosting (xgboost), and a stacked ensemble.
Runs k-fold cross-validation and reports predictive ability (correlation between predicted and observed values on held-out folds), with a bar chart and table.
Fit a final GBLUP to obtain genomic estimated breeding values (GEBVs) and the estimated genomic heritability (h²); export the GEBVs to CSV.
Requires a phenotype column.




Cross-validated predictive ability by model




Kinship
Computes the genomic relationship matrix (GRM) among individuals using the VanRaden method (via GSbench). Shows a clustered heatmap of pairwise relationships and the distribution of off-diagonal (relatedness) and diagonal (1 + inbreeding) coefficients; exports the GRM to CSV. A minimum minor-allele frequency filters out rare markers before the calculation.


Linkage disequilibrium (LD)
Computes pairwise r² between markers, shown as an LD heatmap, with an LD decay plot of r² against marker separation. The number of markers can be capped for very large datasets.

title: User Manual
text:
Reproducibility & citation
Analyses use established methods from the population-genetics and genomic- prediction literature, implemented on top of base R, ape, vegan, and GSbench. If GenoSuite contributes to a publication, please cite it (see the License page).

title: User Manual
text:
Support
Questions, bugs, and feature requests: github.com/mqfarooqi1/GenoSuite/issues.

title: GenoSuite
text:
Modern numerical-genomics analytics — a desktop application for multivariate analysis of genetic marker data, in the spirit of NTSYSpc but rebuilt for the genomic era.

Download User Manual Source on GitHub

title: GenoSuite
text:
What it does
GenoSuite turns SNP / marker tables into publication-ready multivariate analyses through a point-and-click interface — no coding required. It runs as a self-contained Windows application: install one file and launch a graphical workspace.


Distance & clustering
Genetic distance matrices (Euclidean, Manhattan, correlation, Jaccard, Gower) and dendrograms by UPGMA, Ward, and neighbour-joining.


Ordination
Interactive PCA and PCoA with scree plots, coloured by population.


Diversity & structure
Per-marker MAF, heterozygosity, PIC, and population differentiation (Nei’s Fst).


GWAS
Single-marker association with PC structure correction; Manhattan and QQ plots.


Genomic prediction
Cross-validated GBLUP and machine-learning models (elastic net, random forest, gradient boosting, stacked ensemble) with genomic breeding values.


Mantel test
Permutation-based correlation between two distance matrices.


Kinship & LD
Genomic relationship matrix (VanRaden) and pairwise linkage disequilibrium (r²) with decay.

title: GenoSuite
text:
A look inside






Population structure (PCA)







Neighbour-joining tree









GWAS Manhattan plot







Genomic prediction accuracy

title: GenoSuite
text:
Quick start

Download and install GenoSuite (or run it from source).
Launch it — a workspace opens in your browser.
On the Data tab, click Load demo SNP dataset (or upload your own CSV).
Explore the Distance, Clustering, Ordination, Diversity, GWAS, and Prediction tabs.

See the User Manual for full details on every module and the expected data format.

GenoSuite is free, open-source software released under the MIT License. © 2026 Muhammad Farooqi.

title: Download
text:
GenoSuite ships as a self-contained Windows installer — it bundles everything it needs, so no separate R installation is required.
Download GenoSuite-Setup.exe
The latest installer is published on the GitHub Releases page.






Note



The installer is large (~200–300 MB) because it includes a private copy of R and all required packages. This is what makes it self-contained: install once, run offline.





Download GenoSuite-Setup.exe.
Double-click it and follow the prompts (you may see a Windows SmartScreen notice for a new publisher — choose More info → Run anyway).
Launch GenoSuite from the Start menu.





Windows 10 or 11 (64-bit)
A modern web browser (Edge, Chrome, or Firefox) for the interface
~700 MB free disk space after installation

title: Download
text:
Run from source
Developers and macOS/Linux users can run GenoSuite from source with R:
install.packages(c("shiny", "bslib", "DT", "ggplot2", "ape", "vegan",
                   "glmnet", "ranger", "xgboost"))
install.packages("GSbench", repos = "https://mqfarooqi1.r-universe.dev")

# from the cloned repository
shiny::runApp("app")
The full source is on GitHub under the MIT License.

title: License & Copyright
text:
Third-party components
The self-contained installer bundles, and GenoSuite depends on, third-party open-source software that remains under its own licence, including:

The R language and environment — GNU General Public License (GPL-2 / GPL-3).
R packages: shiny, bslib, DT, ggplot2 (MIT/GPL), ape, vegan (GPL-2), glmnet, ranger, xgboost, and GSbench (MIT).

These components are redistributed in accordance with their respective licences. Because the distribution includes GPL-licensed software (R), the bundled installer as a whole is distributed under terms compatible with the GPL; the GenoSuite source code itself is offered under the MIT License above.

text:

If GenoSuite contributes to a publication, please cite it as:
